steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks for the most logical transition between two sentences. Sentence 1 states that Cairo Metro residents rely on the system for millions of trips each year. Sentence 2 states that city officials strive to maintain the system’s 71 stations. The decisive clue is the direction of the relationship: does sentence 2 contrast with, follow from, illustrate, or offer an alternative to sentence 1?

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — For this reason,: signals cause-and-effect — sentence 2 is the result of sentence 1. Heavy ridership (cause) explains why officials work to maintain the stations (effect). This relationship fits the logic of the passage. ✓
  • Choice B — However,: signals contrast. There is no contradiction between high ridership and officials maintaining stations — both point in the same direction. ✗
  • Choice C — For example,: signals that sentence 2 is a specific instance of a general claim made in sentence 1. But maintenance of stations is not an example of millions of trips; it is a consequence of them. ✗
  • Choice D — Alternatively,: signals a different option or substitute. Maintaining stations is not an alternative to heavy ridership; it is a response to it. ✗
A is correct.
